value: Context engineering
Sources, tokens, manipulation, hallucinations
Sources (RAG), deep research, and tokens – how to control context and get more accurate answers.
You already know how to write prompts. Now – context and reliability.
In this module: sources, data, and checking answers
After this module you'll have fewer errors and hallucinations in AI answers – you'll control sources and verification.
You already know how to write prompts.
Now – context and reliability.
In this module you'll learn to add sources to prompts and check answers. RAG (answers from your sources – we'll explain later), hallucinations – how to work smarter.
See what's ahead – in 1 minute!
Write me a report on Lithuania's GDP trends.

META: You are an economics analyst. Goal: a short GDP trends report.

INPUT: Use only these sources: Eurostat, Statistics Lithuania. If data is missing – say "I don't know", don't guess.

OUTPUT: 1 page, 3–5 bullet points, with links to sources. Language: English.

Once the 6 blocks are familiar, RAG (answers from your documents and sources) and fact-checking let you work with real data and reduce errors. Token economy – save time and cost; spotting manipulation – ethical, reliable use. This module prepares you for one integrated project (Module 6).
RAG and sources – specify sources in the prompt and reduce errors
Deep research – structure multi-step queries with AI
Tokens and verification – optimise length and check answer accuracy
Manipulation and hallucinations – spot and avoid; neutral prompts
Apply it all in one project (Module 6)

Artificial intelligence and the universe: how it all connects
AI – much more than one tool or platform.
As Dante pictured the world in circles – so here: the deeper you go, the more powerful it gets.
Dante's universe (Divine Comedy) – 14th c.
Dante pictures the world as a hierarchy where each level has its own rules, limits, and purpose.
Dante Alighieri, Divina Commedia (Divine Comedy), c. 1308–1321.
AI universe
Artificial intelligence is a broad field. The deeper you go, the more specialised, complex, and powerful the systems become.
Various educational AI schemas (IBM, Google, NVIDIA). Summary schema – 2023–2025.
1️⃣ In short (30 s)

AI isn't just ChatGPT. You'll see where generative AI sits in the AI universe, and why it matters before diving into context engineering.

**Benefit:** One prompt – and AI explains the whole hierarchy with examples.
🔽 Want more detail? Why start with Dante?
It helps to see that AI has many levels – that will make it clearer later where sources and context "fit". Next – slide 3: Context engineering: how to steer AI.
2️⃣ Do it now (2–7 min)

**What to do:** Copy the prompt below, paste it into any AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) and run it.

🔘 **Copy prompt (below)**

**What you get:** An AI hierarchy (AI → ML → Neural networks → Deep learning → Generative AI) with real-life examples.
3️⃣ Copyable prompt
Paste this prompt into your AI tool and run:

META: You are an educational assistant explaining the AI hierarchy in plain language.

INPUT: Explain the AI universe – the hierarchy from broadest to narrowest. Use a metaphor (e.g. Dante's Divine Comedy universe) if it helps.

OUTPUT:
• Schema: AI → Machine learning → Neural networks → Deep learning → Generative AI
• 1–2 sentences per level + a real-life example
• Why ChatGPT is just one layer, not all of AI
• Language: [your choice]
4️⃣ Check (1 min)

Review the AI answer and ask yourself:

• Is the **schema** clear – from broadest to narrowest?
• Does each **level** have a real-life example?
• Is it clear why **ChatGPT ≠ all of AI**?
• Could you **explain it to a colleague** in 1 minute?

**If 2 or more "no" → go back to the prompt and add to the INPUT; don't rewrite the whole prompt.**
🔽 Key message

Both universes work the same way: **the deeper you go – the more power, but also more responsibility.**

• Not all AI is ChatGPT – that's one of the most common mix-ups
• Generative AI is just one, deepest part of the AI universe
• The metaphor helps you see "where we are" and prepares you for what comes next
🔽 Terms (1): AI, machine learning, neural networks

**Artificial intelligence (AI)** – broad field: computer systems that do tasks that usually need human thinking. Example: an email spam filter that learns to tell spam from real mail.

**Machine learning (ML)** – part of AI that learns from data, not fixed rules. Example: Netflix recommends films based on what you watched – no one programmed each recommendation.

**Neural networks** – models inspired by the brain: many nodes (neurons) in layers. They can handle images, sound, text, etc. (multimodal). Example: your phone's face unlock – a neural network learned to recognise your face from thousands of points.
🔽 Terms (2): Deep learning, generative AI

**Deep learning** – advanced, many-layer neural networks (10–100+ layers). They find complex patterns in large data. Example: self-driving car vision – the system sees signs, pedestrians, cars at once.

**Generative AI** – AI that **creates new content**: text, images, sound, music. This is the deepest part of the universe – where ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, DALL-E and the tools in this module live. Example: you write a prompt – AI generates an article, slides, or image.
Next – slide 3: Context engineering: how to steer AI
🧩 Context engineering: how to "steer" AI
What it is, why it matters – and what OpenAI's research shows
1️⃣ In short (15 s)
Give AI a clear role, format, and limits – and you get better answers. Try the template below.
Why this?
After the AI universe we lock in the main idea: **context = your steering wheel.** Below – how it works and why it matters. This sets you up for RAG, sources, and checking answers.
How do LLMs work?

Large language models (LLMs) generate text **word by word**: input → LLM → probabilities for the next word → chosen token. Each word depends on **all previous context** – so a well-shaped prompt (role, sources, format) cuts errors and hallucination risk.

Autoregressive language model sketch: step N (input "Rytas tapo", output with percentages: čempionais, 2024, etc., chosen "čempionais") and N+1 (input "Rytas tapo čempionais", chosen "2024"); example Rytas, LKL
What is context engineering?

It's a way to **give AI the information it needs** so it understands:

• what its role is,
• what it should do,
• what result you expect.

**In plain terms:** not a question to AI, but **explaining the situation** to AI.
What does context include?

• **Goal** – what we want to get
• **Role** – how AI should think
• **Limits** – what not to do
• **Extra information** – data, examples
2️⃣ InstructGPT: what the research shows (illustration)

**What the study shows:** better context (role, format, limits) = better ratings from evaluators. Below – results from OpenAI's experiment.

Role + format + limits = roughly twice the perceived quality. Models that follow instructions better give higher-quality answers – the basis for RAG and fact-checking.
What was measured? Result

OpenAI measured **output quality** (Likert 1–7 – rating scale; SFT = learning from human examples). **InstructGPT** clearly leads: smaller (1.3B) InstructGPT is rated better than larger (175B) GPT-3 without alignment – so **role, format, and limits (context engineering)** matter more than model size. **Module 4 takeaway:** when you specify role, format, and limits, AI follows instructions better – and that helps you use RAG and check answers later.
In one sentence
**Good context → better AI decisions.** AI is powerful, but without context it guesses.
How does that help?

• Fewer errors and "hallucinations"
• More accurate, predictable answers
• AI becomes a work tool, not a toy
• You can use it for automation and processes, not just one-off answers
Try it now (1 min)
Copy the template below into ChatGPT or Claude, fill in the bracketed parts and run – that's already minimal context. Example: [your role] = "business analyst", [one sentence] = "prepare a summary".
You are [your role, e.g. business analyst]. My task today: [one sentence]. What format I want: [e.g. 5 bullet points, English].
Action: what to do
**When writing prompts – specify role, format, and limits.** Try it in the next Module 4 slides or in the prompt library.
Sources

1) OpenAI (2022). Instruction following – quality ratings by model size. [openai.com/index/instruction-following](https://openai.com/index/instruction-following/).
2) Autoregressive LLM sketch: [RBC Borealis – A high-level overview of LLMs](https://rbcborealis.com/research-blogs/a-high-level-overview-of-large-language-models/).
A 1.3B InstructGPT model is rated better than 175B GPT-3 by evaluators – that shows alignment strategy, not parameter count, is the main driver of perceived quality.
Evaluator ratings when comparing model outputs. Higher = better instruction following.
InstructGPT top
Likert 175B

Supervised fine-tuning 175B
GPT with prompt

4 ingredients of a good prompt
Four angles on good prompts – and what that means in practice
From prompt to context: one pipeline, two paths

Prompt engineering → prompt straight to model.
Context engineering → we add context and tools.
Short explanation
Example prompt
Without context
With context
This step is active only in Context engineering mode.
Prompt engineering path
Short path: goal → prompt → model → answer. Context nodes off.
Context engineering path
Full path: prompt and context form the input; the LLM uses tools when needed.
User's goal
Say clearly what result you need.
Here you define what you want: ideas, plan, text, or analysis.
Prepare 5 marketing ideas for a logistics company in Lithuania.
Answer is generic – it doesn't know your business situation.
Answer is aimed at a clear goal and audience.
Prompt
Instruction to the model: clear, short, unambiguous.
The prompt says what the model should do and what format you expect.
Create 5 ideas. For each give a title, channel, and short explanation.
Ideas are generic, often like common templates.
Prompt uses extra information, so suggestions are more targeted.
Context
Add facts: documents, CRM data, rules, limits.
Context reduces guessing. The model uses what you give, not vague assumptions.
Context: B2B logistics, 40 staff, goal – new clients in Klaipėda, budget 1500 EUR/month.
Model doesn't know your real situation and suggests "one size fits all" options.
Model picks ideas to match your data, limits, and priorities.
LLM
Model analyses, combines information, generates the answer.
Processing happens here: model reads the prompt, uses context, and shapes the result.
Give the answer in a table: idea | why it fits logistics | first step.
Result is smooth but less practical for real work.
Result is more actionable, with clearer next steps.
Tools / data
When you need facts, add search, files, DB, or other tools.
Tools let the model use up-to-date or internal data that isn't in the prompt.
Check the last 3 months' sales CSV and suggest ideas for the TOP client segment.
No check against real data, so the answer can be too abstract.
Answer is based on real numbers and more reliable.
Output
Final result: plan, answer, or document.
Output shows how well the pipeline was set up: better context → more useful result.
At the end add 3 actions we can do this week.
Good to start, but often needs a lot of manual tweaking.
Easier to use in practice, fewer fixes.
1️⃣ In short (1–2 min)
In 1–2 min you'll rate your prompt on 4 criteria and get 1–2 tips. Copy the evaluator prompt below, paste your prompt, and get clear feedback.
Why this?
A good prompt rests on 4 ingredients: goal, context, structure, and format. When they're clear, AI answers more accurately and practically.
1. Engineering

One try isn't enough: set success criteria, test, and improve.

**In practice:** a few targeted tweaks beat lots of one-off prompts.
2. Language

Clear words and concrete context reduce guessing.

**In practice:** changing the wording can change the result a lot.
3. Psychology

Framing and audience shape how the task is understood.

**In practice:** say who the answer is for and you get more focused output.
4. Communication

Direct instructions and clear format make the answer predictable.

**In practice:** write the steps and the format you want.
Bottom line

All four work together: goal and iteration (engineering), clarity and context (language), audience (psychology), directness and format (communication). A good prompt isn't just "what I wrote" – it's whether a colleague would have understood it.
2️⃣ Do it now (1–2 min)

**What to do:** copy the evaluator prompt below and paste it into AI (ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini). Then paste your prompt and get a short rating on 4 criteria.

🔘 Copy prompt (below)
3️⃣ Copyable prompt (evaluator)
**One prompt evaluates another:** run the evaluator first, then paste your prompt. You get a 4-criteria rating and 1–2 tips.

META: You are a prompt quality evaluator. You rate on 4 criteria: goal, context/audience, structure, format.

INPUT: A single prompt will be pasted below. Reply briefly (1–2 sentences each): 1) Goal 2) Context and audience 3) Structure 4) Format.

OUTPUT: After the 4 points give 1–2 concrete tips (what to add or change). English, concise.
Steps: one prompt evaluates another
Steps: one prompt evaluates another

1. Copy the evaluator prompt from the block above (3️⃣).
2. Paste it into AI (ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini).
3. Paste your prompt.
4. Get a short rating on 4 criteria and 1–2 tips.

The evaluator helps you improve your prompt.
System prompt and Master prompt: the difference
System = AI rules; Master = your context. Together – consistent answers.
Process prompt: mapping your workflow
Define your AI workflow – steps you reuse. Optional.
Process gives clarity to teams and decision-makers; a defined workflow is "tracks for the train" for later chains and tools.
Working with AI: structured process
Structured process
One logic for all tasks: input → processing → result

Knowledge check: spot the AI universe layer
Read 5 examples and for each choose **one correct layer** from the 5 terms. One best answer per example. (2–3 min)
An email app automatically sorts messages into folders by predefined rules.
A bank system automatically flags suspicious transactions.
A program learns to recognise objects in photos from thousands of examples.
A voice-controlled system understands what the user said.
ChatGPT generates an email text for a client.
Artificial intelligence
Machine learning
Neural networks
Deep learning
Generative AI
Works by fixed rules, doesn't learn from data – that's the broader AI field.
System learns from data (e.g. past transactions) and spots anomalies – typical machine learning.
Learning from thousands of examples and complex images – typical of deep learning (many-layer networks).
Speech and sound recognition – neural networks (often multimodal).
Creates new text – that's generative AI (not just recognition).
To see that **not all AI is generative** and that ChatGPT is just **one layer** in the whole AI universe.

Method vs agent prompt: the difference
Method = give the method; agent = AI acts as an agent with tools
1️⃣ In short (30 s)
**Method** = AI writes to your instructions (role, steps, format). **Agent** = AI acts as an agent – searches, gathers, uses tools. Below – how to tell them apart and when to use which.

Is your prompt good… or does it just look that way?
5 principles show the real picture – you can rate it.
1️⃣ In short (30 s)
Principles are the base for the next steps. Copy the prompt below, paste yours – get a rating and 3 weak spots.
2️⃣ Do it now (2–7 min)

**What to do:**
👉 Copy the prompt below and run it in AI (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini). Paste **your** prompt into the INPUT slot – get a rating on 5 principles and a suggested improved version.

🔘 Copy prompt (below)

**Don't aim for perfect** – paste any current prompt you have.
3️⃣ Copyable prompt
Paste your prompt into INPUT – AI will rate it on 5 principles and suggest improvements.
4️⃣ Check (1 min)

• Did you get a **rating table** (1–5 per principle)?
• Are the **3 weak spots** clear and fixable?
• Is the **improved version** useful – could you use it?
• Would you **use** these principles next time before sending a prompt?

If 2 or more "no" – go back to your prompt (INPUT) or change it and run again.
Practice: the prompt below breaks 5 principles. Spot the issues and rewrite for clarity, context, and action verbs – or press "Show solution" and compare.
